Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, on stable antihypertensive therapy
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 3 years ago
Lumbar spondylosis with chronic lower back pain

Mother died of ischemic stroke at age 80
No family history of brain tumors or primary neurological disease

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 15 pack-years, quit 10 years ago

social_history.exercise: Previously walked 30 minutes daily; ceased 6 weeks ago due to unsteadiness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Mildly slowed in responses but coherent. Speech is notable for moderate dysarthria — slurred consonants and reduced articulatory precision, particularly on repetition of rapid phrases. Comprehension intact for simple and complex commands. Naming intact. Attention is adequate — performs months of the year backward with one error. Memory is 3/3 on immediate recall, 2/3 at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements demonstrate a subtle right internuclear ophthalmoplegia with incomplete adduction of the right eye on left lateral gaze and abducting nystagmus of the left eye. No ptosis. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild right peripheral facial weakness (lower face only), which the patient reports developed over the past 3 weeks. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Gag reflex present. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 4+/5 grip strength, otherwise 5/5 proximally. Right lower extremity: 4/5 hip flexion, 4/5 knee extension, 4-/5 ankle dorsiflexion, 4/5 plantarflexion. Left upper extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left lower extremity: 3+/5 hip flexion, 3/5 knee extension, 2+/5 ankle dorsiflexion, 3/5 plantarflexion. No fasciculations. Mild bilateral lower limb spasticity, worse on the left. Increased tone in both lower extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch diminished over the left lower extremity below the knee. Pinprick reduced on the left foot and lateral calf. Proprioception impaired at the left great toe (2/5 correct). Vibration sense reduced at both ankles. No sensory level identified on truncal examination.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk). Achilles 3+ on the right, 3+ on the left with 3 beats of clonus. Left plantar response is extensor (positive Babinski). Right plantar response is equivocal.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing: mild right-sided dysmetria with intention tremor. Left finger-to-nose: unable to perform accurately, marked dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing: markedly impaired bilaterally, left worse than right, with significant ataxia. Rapid alternating movements slowed bilaterally, worse on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate independently. Requires bilateral support. Wide-based, lurching gait with tendency to fall to the left. Tandem gait not attempted due to safety concerns. Romberg not reliably tested due to inability to stand unsupported.
neurological_exam.additional: No papilledema on fundoscopic examination. No meningismus. Karnofsky performance status estimated at 30-40. The combination of dysarthria, bilateral lower limb weakness with left predominance, bilateral ataxia, and cranial nerve findings is most suggestive of a brainstem localization.

patient.chief_complaint: Two months of progressive dizziness, worsening weakness in both legs, and difficulty walking
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Takeshi Yamamoto is a 67-year-old right-handed retired mechanical engineer who presents to the inpatient neurology service with a two-month history of progressive dizziness, bilateral lower extremity weakness, and gait instability. The symptoms began insidiously approximately two months ago with a persistent sense of dizziness that he described as a constant feeling of imbalance rather than true vertigo. He did not experience any spinning sensation, but felt as though the ground was unsteady beneath him. Within the first two weeks, he noticed difficulty ascending stairs, initially attributing this to his chronic lower back pain. However, the weakness progressed beyond what he had experienced with his lumbar spondylosis, and his wife observed that he was shuffling his feet and stumbling, particularly to the left side. Over the following weeks, the weakness became more pronounced. He began having difficulty rising from a seated position without arm support, and approximately six weeks ago he experienced his first fall while walking in his garden, landing on his left side without significant injury. Since then, he has fallen three additional times. His speech has become increasingly slurred over the past month, which initially concerned his wife that he might be having strokes. He was seen at a community hospital three weeks ago where an initial non-contrast CT of the head was read as unremarkable, and his symptoms were attributed to possible progression of his lumbar spondylosis with cervical involvement. He was prescribed a course of physical therapy. However, his condition continued to deteriorate, and over the past two weeks he has become essentially wheelchair-bound, unable to walk more than a few steps even with a walker. He also reports intermittent diplopia, particularly when looking to the left, which he noticed approximately ten days ago. He denies headaches, nausea, vomiting, or visual obscurations. He denies seizure activity, loss of consciousness, or cognitive changes. His appetite has decreased over the past month, with an unintentional weight loss of approximately 5 kg. His wife notes that he has become somewhat withdrawn and less engaged in conversation, though his comprehension and memory appear preserved. He was referred to the university hospital for further evaluation, and brain MRI was ordered by the admitting team.

background.sleep_architecture: Brief drowsiness captured; normal vertex waves

findings.frequency: Intermittent bursts of diffuse theta activity, 5-6 Hz
findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific; may reflect subcortical or deep midline pathology, metabolic factors, or medication effects
Could represent a posterior fossa lesion projecting to the overlying cortex, or could be artifactual given proximity to posterior electrodes. Clinical correlation recommended.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's limited cooperation

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG showing intermittent diffuse theta slowing and intermittent right posterior temporal-occipital focal slowing. No definite epileptiform discharges identified. The diffuse slowing is non-specific and may be seen with subcortical pathology, metabolic disturbance, or medication effect. The right posterior focal slowing warrants correlation with neuroimaging. This EEG does not clearly lateralize or localize a lesion and is of limited diagnostic specificity in isolation.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. Hyperventilation not performed. Limited drowsiness captured. Patient cooperation limited by dysarthria and motor impairment.
recommended_actions: Consider repeat EEG if seizures are clinically suspected
Monitor for subclinical seizure activity if mental status changes

findings.location: Right pons, centered in the ventral and lateral pontine tegmentum, with extension into the right middle cerebellar peduncle

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense to isointense relative to normal pontine parenchyma
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneously hyperintense with areas of mixed signal
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, with surrounding edema extending into the right middle cerebellar peduncle and dorsal pons
signal_characteristics.DWI: No definite restricted diffusion centrally; subtle facilitated diffusion in portions of the lesion. Mildly elevated signal at the enhancing periphery on high b-value images
signal_characteristics.Post-contrast T1: Marked heterogeneous enhancement with irregular borders; enhancement is predominantly solid without a classic ring pattern; no central necrosis identified

findings.mass_effect: Compression of the fourth ventricle with mild rightward displacement. Mild effacement of the prepontine cistern. No obstructive hydrocephalus. Brainstem is expanded on the right side.
findings.borders: Irregular and poorly defined, particularly at the interface with the middle cerebellar peduncle and dorsal pons. The lesion appears infiltrative without a clear capsule.

findings.location: Small chronic lacunar infarct in the left basal ganglia (putamen)

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hypointense centrally with thin hyperintense rim

volumetrics.midline_shift: 0 mm supratentorially; fourth ventricle mildly displaced

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions in the supratentorial compartment or cerebellum
Mild generalized cerebral atrophy appropriate for age
Small vessel ischemic changes in the periventricular white matter (Fazekas grade 1)
No evidence of hemorrhage within the lesion
Vertebrobasilar arterial system appears patent on MRA; no significant stenosis or aneurysm
mri.impression: 1. Enhancing intra-axial mass centered in the right pons measuring 3.2 x 2.5 x 2.8 cm with irregular borders, heterogeneous enhancement, and extension into the right middle cerebellar peduncle. The enhancement pattern and infiltrative morphology are concerning for a brainstem glioma, likely high-grade given the degree of enhancement. 2. The differential diagnosis for an enhancing brainstem mass in an adult includes high-grade glioma (anaplastic astrocytoma or glioblastoma), ependymoma, hemangioblastoma, brainstem metastasis, and less likely lymphoma. 3. Incidental small chronic lacunar infarct in the left putamen, likely related to chronic hypertensive small vessel disease. 4. No obstructive hydrocephalus at present, though the fourth ventricle compression warrants monitoring.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: High-grade brainstem glioma (anaplastic astrocytoma or glioblastoma)
differential_by_imaging.key_features: Infiltrative enhancing pontine mass with irregular borders, T2 hyperintensity, extension into middle cerebellar peduncle; adult onset; progressive neurological deterioration
Can occur in the fourth ventricle/brainstem with enhancement; typically more well-circumscribed and exophytic, which is less consistent here
Posterior fossa enhancing lesion; however, typically cystic with mural nodule in the cerebellum; solid pontine hemangioblastomas are uncommon
Solitary enhancing pontine lesion could represent metastasis; however, infiltrative borders favor primary glial neoplasm; no known primary malignancy; former smoker raises concern
Can present as an enhancing brainstem mass; typically more homogeneous enhancement and periventricular; patient is immunocompetent

recommended_actions: MR perfusion to assess relative cerebral blood volume in the enhancing component
MR spectroscopy for metabolite analysis (choline/NAA ratio, lipid/lactate peaks)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to exclude occult primary malignancy given former smoking history
Neurosurgical consultation to discuss stereotactic biopsy feasibility given brainstem location
Consider 3D arterial spin labeling to assess lesion vascularity

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon: 38 mm)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 76 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria, likely related to long-standing hypertension. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmias.
ecg.clinical_correlation: LVH by voltage criteria is an expected finding in a patient with 12-year history of hypertension. No cardiac abnormality to explain the neurological presentation.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly low; may reflect decreased oral intake and nutritional status over recent months
Mildly low, consistent with mild normocytic anemia

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated; patient has known type 2 diabetes on metformin. May reflect stress hyperglycemia or suboptimal glycemic control.

Miscellaneous.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; non-specific. Could reflect underlying malignancy, chronic inflammation, or age-related elevation.
Mildly elevated; non-specific inflammatory marker. Could be related to underlying neoplasm or recent immobility/deconditioning.
Elevated, indicating suboptimal glycemic control in a known diabetic. Baseline for treatment planning.
Within normal range; rules out B12 deficiency as contributing factor to neurological symptoms

labs.interpretation: Mild normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL) possibly related to decreased oral intake or chronic disease. Elevated fasting glucose (148 mg/dL) and HbA1c (7.2%) consistent with known diabetes with suboptimal control. Mildly elevated ESR (32 mm/hr) and CRP (8.4 mg/L) suggesting a low-grade inflammatory or neoplastic process. Hepatic and renal function are normal, providing an adequate baseline for potential chemotherapy. Coagulation studies are normal. TSH and B12 are within normal limits, making metabolic/nutritional causes of the neurological presentation less likely.

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Elevated rCBV ratio of 2.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing pons (ratio >1.75 suggests high-grade neoplasm)
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Increased regional cerebral blood flow within the enhancing portion
signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Elevated permeability (Ktrans = 0.12 min^-1), indicating disrupted blood-brain barrier

additional_observations: Non-enhancing FLAIR-bright regions surrounding the mass show lower perfusion (rCBV ratio 1.1), suggesting vasogenic edema rather than infiltrating tumor in those regions
Perfusion values in the contralateral pons and cerebellum are normal
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates elevated rCBV (ratio 2.8) and increased permeability in the enhancing component of the right pontine lesion, supporting a high-grade neoplastic process. The perfusion characteristics are consistent with a WHO grade III-IV glioma and argue against low-grade glioma, which typically shows lower rCBV values.
recommended_actions: Findings support high-grade neoplasm — tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy recommended
MR spectroscopy for additional metabolic characterization

findings.type: MR spectroscopy — single voxel and multivoxel analysis
findings.location: Right pontine lesion — enhancing solid component

signal_characteristics.Cho_NAA_ratio: 5.3 (markedly elevated; >2.0 highly suggestive of high-grade neoplasm)
signal_characteristics.Lipid_Lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks present, suggesting necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

additional_observations: Spectral pattern is consistent with a high-grade glial neoplasm
Absence of amino acid peaks argues against abscess
Choline elevation extends slightly beyond the enhancing margin, suggesting infiltrating tumor cells in the peritumoral region
output.impression: MR spectroscopy demonstrates a metabolite profile highly consistent with a high-grade glioma: markedly elevated choline (reflecting high membrane turnover/cellularity), markedly reduced NAA (reflecting neuronal loss/destruction), and prominent lipid-lactate peaks (reflecting necrosis and hypoxia). The Cho/NAA ratio of 5.3 is strongly suggestive of WHO grade III or IV glioma.
recommended_actions: Stereotactic biopsy for definitive tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q)

CT_Staging.value: No pulmonary masses or nodules identified. Mild centrilobular emphysema in the upper lobes consistent with former smoking history. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.
CT_Staging.clinical_significance: No evidence of primary lung malignancy or pulmonary metastatic disease

CT_Staging.value: Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands unremarkable. No focal hepatic lesions. Kidneys show bilateral simple cortical cysts (largest 1.8 cm right kidney). No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.
CT_Staging.clinical_significance: No evidence of intra-abdominal primary malignancy or metastatic disease

CT_Staging.value: Mildly enlarged prostate gland consistent with BPH. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease.
CT_Staging.clinical_significance: Benign prostatic enlargement only; no concerning findings

output.interpretation: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no evidence of a primary extracranial malignancy or metastatic disease. Mild emphysematous changes are consistent with the patient's former smoking history. The absence of an extracranial primary tumor supports a primary CNS neoplasm as the diagnosis for the brainstem lesion.
abnormal_values_summary: Mild centrilobular emphysema (upper lobes, non-clinically significant)

Histopathology.value: Hypercellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, cellular pleomorphism, and increased mitotic activity (8 mitoses per 10 high-power fields). No microvascular proliferation. No palisading necrosis. Tumor cells are predominantly astrocytic in morphology with elongated processes on GFAP staining.
Histopathology.clinical_significance: Features diagnostic of anaplastic astrocytoma, WHO grade III. Absence of necrosis and microvascular proliferation argues against grade IV (glioblastoma).
Elevated proliferation index consistent with high-grade (WHO grade III) neoplasm

Molecular_Pathology.value: Wild-type (IDH1 R132H immunostain negative; IDH1/2 sequencing negative)
Molecular_Pathology.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype status in a high-grade astrocytoma confers a worse prognosis and is molecularly consistent with glioblastoma behavior per WHO 2021 classification, even without histological necrosis/microvascular proliferation
Unmethylated MGMT promoter predicts reduced benefit from temozolomide chemotherapy
Absence of 1p/19q co-deletion excludes oligodendroglioma; consistent with astrocytic lineage
Retained ATRX expression, consistent with IDH-wildtype astrocytoma

TERT promoter mutation in an IDH-wildtype diffuse astrocytic glioma meets WHO 2021 criteria for molecular glioblastoma (grade 4), even in the absence of histological necrosis or microvascular proliferation
EGFR amplification further supports molecular classification as glioblastoma per WHO 2021

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy of the right pontine lesion demonstrates a high-grade diffuse astrocytic glioma. Histologically, the tumor shows features of anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO grade III) with increased cellularity, nuclear atypia, and mitotic activity, but without necrosis or microvascular proliferation. However, molecular profiling reveals IDH-wildtype status, TERT promoter mutation (C228T), and EGFR amplification. Per WHO 2021 CNS classification criteria, the presence of TERT promoter mutation and/or EGFR amplification in an IDH-wildtype diffuse astrocytic glioma warrants a diagnosis of glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype (WHO grade 4) regardless of histological grade. MGMT promoter is unmethylated, which has implications for temozolomide responsiveness.
abnormal_values_summary: TERT promoter mutation (C228T) — molecular criterion for glioblastoma grade 4
EGFR amplification — additional molecular criterion for glioblastoma grade 4
Final integrated diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4

findings.type: Functional MRI — motor and language mapping
findings.location: Bilateral motor and language cortices assessed

signal_characteristics.Motor_cortex: Primary motor cortex activation is appropriately lateralized and located in expected precentral gyri bilaterally; no tumor involvement of supratentorial motor cortex
signal_characteristics.Corticospinal_tracts: DTI tractography demonstrates displacement and thinning of the right corticospinal tract by the pontine mass; partial infiltration of the right corticospinal tract fibers
signal_characteristics.Language_areas: Broca's and Wernicke's areas show normal activation patterns; left-hemisphere dominant for language

additional_observations: The pontine lesion involves the right corticospinal tract and right medial lemniscus based on DTI fiber tracking
The left corticospinal tract appears intact but compressed
Surgical resection of the lesion carries high risk of worsening bilateral motor deficits and potential locked-in syndrome
output.impression: Functional MRI with DTI tractography demonstrates involvement and partial infiltration of the right corticospinal tract by the pontine mass. The lesion's brainstem location and involvement of critical white matter tracts make gross total surgical resection extremely high risk. This information is essential for surgical planning and supports a biopsy-only approach with adjuvant chemoradiation.
recommended_actions: Multidisciplinary tumor board discussion regarding treatment options
Consider radiation therapy with concurrent temozolomide as primary treatment given unresectable location
Discuss prognosis and goals of care with patient and family

output.query: brainstem glioma adult treatment high-grade astrocytoma pons

results.title: Adult Brainstem Gliomas: A Critical Review of Surgical Indications and Outcomes
results.summary: Adult brainstem gliomas represent 1-2% of adult brain tumors. Unlike pediatric diffuse intrinsic pontine gliomas (DIPG), adult brainstem gliomas are heterogeneous and may benefit from biopsy-guided treatment. Stereotactic biopsy is recommended for tissue diagnosis with a procedural morbidity of 3-6%. Gross total resection is rarely feasible for intrinsic pontine tumors.

results.title: Molecular Classification Redefines Adult Brainstem Gliomas: IDH Status and TERT Promoter Mutations
results.summary: IDH-wildtype brainstem gliomas with TERT promoter mutations behave as molecular glioblastomas regardless of histological grade. These patients have a median overall survival of 12-15 months with standard chemoradiation, compared to >36 months for IDH-mutant tumors.

results.title: Radiotherapy for Brainstem Gliomas in Adults: Dose Optimization and Outcomes
results.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics
results.summary: Standard external beam radiation therapy (54-60 Gy in 30-33 fractions) with concurrent temozolomide remains the standard of care for high-grade brainstem gliomas in adults. Hypofractionated regimens (40 Gy in 15 fractions) may be considered for elderly patients or those with poor performance status.

output.summary: Adult brainstem high-grade gliomas are rare but aggressive tumors. Stereotactic biopsy is recommended over resection for intrinsic pontine tumors. IDH-wildtype status with TERT promoter mutation confers glioblastoma-equivalent prognosis (median OS 12-15 months). Standard treatment is radiation (54-60 Gy) with concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide, though benefit of temozolomide is limited in MGMT-unmethylated tumors. Hypofractionated radiation may be appropriate for patients with poor functional status.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m^2 daily during radiation, then 150-200 mg/m^2 for 5 days every 28 days
interactions: Metformin + Temozolomide: No direct pharmacokinetic interaction. However, temozolomide may cause nausea/vomiting that could affect oral metformin absorption. Monitor blood glucose closely during treatment.
Aspirin + Temozolomide: Temozolomide causes thrombocytopenia; aspirin increases bleeding risk. Consider holding aspirin during temozolomide cycles if platelet count drops below 100 x10^9/L.
contraindications: Severe myelosuppression (ANC <1.5 x10^9/L or platelets <100 x10^9/L) — dose reduction or treatment delay required
Known hypersensitivity to temozolomide or dacarbazine
warnings: MGMT promoter is unmethylated — reduced expected benefit from temozolomide. Discuss with patient regarding risk-benefit ratio.
Hepatic function must be monitored — temozolomide is hepatically metabolized. Baseline liver function is normal.

Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is standard during concurrent chemoradiation — NOTE: patient has sulfonamide allergy. Use pentamidine inhalation or dapsone as alternative.
output.formulary_status: Formulary approved; requires neuro-oncology attending authorization
alternatives: Bevacizumab — may be considered at recurrence or for radiation necrosis
Tumor treating fields (TTFields/Optune) — standard for supratentorial GBM; limited data for brainstem tumors
Lomustine (CCNU) — alternative alkylating agent for MGMT-unmethylated tumors
Clinical trial enrollment — consider for this difficult-to-treat location

signal_characteristics.CBF: Increased cerebral blood flow in the right pons corresponding to the enhancing lesion (CBF = 68 mL/100g/min vs. 32 mL/100g/min in contralateral normal pons)

additional_observations: Increased CBF on ASL is consistent with tumor neovascularity and supports a high-grade neoplasm
ASL provides a non-contrast assessment of tumor perfusion that corroborates the DSC perfusion findings
output.impression: 3D arterial spin labeling demonstrates significantly increased cerebral blood flow (CBF ratio 2.1) in the right pontine lesion, consistent with tumor neovascularity and supporting the diagnosis of a high-grade glioma. This finding mirrors the elevated rCBV seen on DSC perfusion imaging.
recommended_actions: Findings reinforce high-grade neoplasm; proceed with tissue diagnosis
ASL can be used for treatment monitoring to assess response to chemoradiation

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4 (brainstem — pontine location)

differential.diagnosis: Anaplastic astrocytoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade III
differential.likelihood: Initially histologically consistent, but molecular features (TERT, EGFR) upgrade to GBM per WHO 2021
differential.key_distinguishing: Histological absence of necrosis/microvascular proliferation initially suggested grade III; however, IDH-wildtype status with TERT promoter mutation and EGFR amplification meet WHO 2021 molecular criteria for grade 4 glioblastoma
Ependymomas in the brainstem tend to arise from the fourth ventricle floor and are more well-circumscribed; this lesion is infiltrative and intra-axial
Typically cystic with enhancing mural nodule in the cerebellum; solid pontine hemangioblastomas are rare; no association with VHL syndrome clinically
Former smoker raises concern; however, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis negative for primary malignancy; infiltrative borders favor primary CNS tumor over metastasis which typically has well-defined margins
Typically shows homogeneous enhancement and periventricular predilection; patient is immunocompetent; biopsy rules this out

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the brainstem lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Enhancing intra-axial right pontine mass with infiltrative borders suggestive of brainstem glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, hepatic panel, coagulation, and inflammatory markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild anemia, elevated glucose (known DM), mildly elevated ESR/CRP; normal hepatic and renal function for treatment baseline

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to assess for subclinical seizure activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse slowing and right posterior focal slowing without clear epileptiform discharges; non-localizing but consistent with subcortical pathology

optimal_actions.action: Request MR perfusion to assess tumor grade non-invasively
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV ratio (2.8) in the enhancing component suggesting high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy to evaluate tumor metabolite profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Markedly elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid-lactate peaks — consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out primary malignancy (metastasis workup), especially given former smoking history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of extracranial primary malignancy, supporting primary CNS neoplasm diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurosurgery for stereotactic biopsy with molecular profiling
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Histology shows anaplastic astrocytoma features; molecular profiling reveals IDH-wildtype, TERT promoter mutation, EGFR amplification — integrated diagnosis: glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on adult brainstem glioma treatment options
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard treatment is radiation with concurrent temozolomide; limited surgical options for intrinsic pontine tumors; poor prognosis for IDH-wildtype tumors

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for temozolomide with patient's current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Note aspirin-temozolomide bleeding risk with thrombocytopenia; sulfonamide allergy requires alternative PJP prophylaxis; MGMT unmethylated status limits temozolomide benefit

optimal_actions.action: Request functional MRI with DTI tractography for surgical risk assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right corticospinal tract infiltrated; gross total resection carries unacceptable risk of locked-in syndrome; supports biopsy-only approach

critical_actions: Obtain contrast-enhanced brain MRI to characterize the brainstem lesion
Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or appropriate staging workup to rule out metastasis, especially given former smoking history
Obtain tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q)
Recognize that IDH-wildtype status with TERT promoter mutation upgrades diagnosis to glioblastoma WHO grade 4 per 2021 WHO classification
Address sulfonamide allergy when planning PJP prophylaxis during chemoradiation — use pentamidine or dapsone instead of TMP-SMX
Recognize that the brainstem location makes gross total resection infeasible and plan for chemoradiation as primary treatment
contraindicated_actions: Attempting gross total surgical resection of an intrinsic pontine lesion — unacceptable risk of devastating neurological morbidity
Administering trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) for PJP prophylaxis in a patient with documented sulfonamide allergy
Starting high-dose corticosteroids before biopsy in a case where CNS lymphoma is in the differential — steroids can lyse lymphoma cells and render biopsy non-diagnostic
Delaying tissue diagnosis by relying solely on imaging — molecular profiling fundamentally changes the diagnosis and prognosis in this case
Administering lumbar puncture without imaging assessment of posterior fossa mass effect and potential risk of tonsillar herniation
key_reasoning_points: An enhancing brainstem mass in an adult has a broad differential including high-grade glioma, ependymoma, hemangioblastoma, metastasis, and lymphoma — tissue diagnosis is essential
The infiltrative borders, T2 hyperintensity, and extension into the middle cerebellar peduncle favor a primary glial neoplasm over metastasis or ependymoma
MR perfusion (elevated rCBV) and spectroscopy (high choline, low NAA, lipid/lactate peaks) provide non-invasive evidence of high-grade neoplasm prior to biopsy
The former smoking history is a red herring that raises concern for metastasis, but CT staging is negative and the infiltrative morphology favors a primary CNS tumor

Per WHO 2021 classification, the presence of TERT promoter mutation and EGFR amplification in an IDH-wildtype diffuse astrocytic glioma warrants a diagnosis of glioblastoma grade 4, even though histology alone shows grade III (anaplastic astrocytoma) features
The incidental lacunar infarct in the left putamen is related to chronic hypertension and is not responsible for the patient's current progressive symptoms
The mildly elevated ESR and CRP are non-specific inflammatory markers that can be seen with malignancy and should not divert attention toward infectious or autoimmune etiologies
Brainstem location fundamentally limits surgical options — stereotactic biopsy is the appropriate surgical intervention, not resection
MGMT promoter unmethylated status suggests limited benefit from temozolomide, but concurrent chemoradiation remains the standard of care

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: The brainstem location is atypical for classic GBM presentation, the initial histology suggests grade III (anaplastic astrocytoma) but molecular profiling upgrades to grade 4, former smoking history introduces a red herring for metastasis, and the incidental lacunar infarct and mildly elevated inflammatory markers add confounders. The case requires integration of advanced imaging, histopathology, and molecular data to arrive at the correct WHO 2021 integrated diagnosis.
